county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Municipio of Lajas, established 1883, carved from San Germán. Puerto Rico is a US territory (since 1898); previously a Spanish colony from 1508. Each municipio functions as a county-equivalent jurisdiction.
county_metadata.research_tips: Lajas was carved from San Germán in 1883 in the southwestern Lajas Valley. Known as 'La Ciudad de la Piña' and for the Laguna Cartagena wildlife refuge area. Parroquia Inmaculada Concepción holds records from the 1880s. Pre-1883 records are in San Germán, one of Puerto Rico's two founding towns. The Lajas Valley (Valle de Lajas) irrigation project in the 20th century transformed agriculture — project records document land redistribution affecting many families. Sugar cultivation generated enslaved persons records before abolition in 1873. Check San Germán's extensive colonial-era church and notarial records for pre-separation families. Civil registry from 1885 overlaps with municipio formation.

source_profiles.source_name: Lajas Catholic Parish Registers (Baptisms, Marriages, Burials)
source_profiles.repository: Parish Archives / FamilySearch / AGPR, San Juan
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Catholic parish registers for Lajas municipio including baptisms (bautismos), marriages (matrimonios), and burials (defunciones). FamilySearch has digitized microfilm for many Puerto Rico parishes. Coverage begins circa 1883.
source_profiles.survival_notes: Parish registers are the primary genealogical source for Puerto Rico. Some early volumes deteriorated due to tropical climate. FamilySearch microfilm provides backup copies.

source_profiles.source_name: Lajas Civil Registry (Registro Demográfico) — Births, Marriages, Deaths
source_profiles.repository: Registro Demográfico, Lajas / AGPR, San Juan
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Civil registry records for Lajas including births, marriages, and deaths from 1885. Spanish civil registration began 1885 under Royal Decree. US administration continued the system after 1898.
source_profiles.survival_notes: Civil registry records generally well-preserved from 1885. Some gaps in early years due to incomplete compliance. Tropical climate and hurricanes have affected some volumes.

source_profiles.source_name: Lajas Notarial Protocols (Protocolos Notariales)
source_profiles.repository: Archivo General de Puerto Rico (AGPR), San Juan
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Notarial protocols for Lajas district covering property transfers, testaments, powers of attorney, marriage contracts, slave sales, coartación agreements, and other legal instruments from 1883. Spanish legal tradition required notarized documents for most transactions.
source_profiles.survival_notes: Notarial protocols at AGPR are generally well-preserved. Some volumes from the earliest periods show deterioration. AGPR's climate-controlled storage protects these irreplaceable records.

source_profiles.source_name: Lajas Spanish Colonial Census Records (Padrones)
source_profiles.repository: Archivo General de Puerto Rico (AGPR), San Juan / Archivo Histórico Nacional, Madrid
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Spanish colonial census records (padrones) for Lajas. Include population counts by race, age, occupation, and household. Key padrones: 1765, 1776, 1800, 1812, 1820, 1846, 1860, 1887, 1897. Some padrones list individuals by name; others are statistical summaries.
source_profiles.survival_notes: Survival varies by year. The 1800, 1860, and 1897 padrones are among the most complete. Some early padrones survive only as summaries at the Archivo Histórico Nacional in Madrid.

source_profiles.source_name: Lajas Property Registry (Registro de la Propiedad)
source_profiles.repository: Registro de la Propiedad, Lajas district
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Property registry records for Lajas district. The Spanish mortgage law system (Ley Hipotecaria) was established in Puerto Rico in 1870, creating a formal property registration system that continues today. Records document property ownership, transfers, mortgages, and liens.
source_profiles.survival_notes: Property registry records are generally well-preserved from 1870. The formal registration system provides continuous chains of title.

source_profiles.source_name: Lajas Municipal Cemetery Records (Cementerio Municipal)
source_profiles.repository: Municipio de Lajas / Find A Grave
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Municipal cemetery records for Lajas. Municipal cemeteries were established in most Puerto Rico municipios during the 19th century under Spanish health regulations. Records include burial registers, grave assignments, and monument inscriptions. Find A Grave has partial crowdsourced transcriptions.
source_profiles.survival_notes: Municipal cemetery records vary in completeness. Earlier burials were in church cemeteries (camposantos). Some municipal cemetery records were damaged by hurricanes.

county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Municipio of Lares, established 1827, carved from San Sebastián, Aguadilla. Puerto Rico is a US territory (since 1898); previously a Spanish colony from 1508. Each municipio functions as a county-equivalent jurisdiction.
county_metadata.research_tips: Lares was carved from San Sebastián and Aguadilla in 1827 in the western mountains. Famous as the site of El Grito de Lares (September 23, 1868), the most significant independence uprising against Spain. Parroquia San José holds records from the 1820s. Military tribunal records, political prisoner lists, and Spanish colonial correspondence about the revolt are at AGPR and Archivo Histórico Nacional in Madrid. Coffee cultivation dominated the economy — hacienda records document highland farming families. Venezuelan, Dominican, and Corsican immigrants participated in the revolt and are documented in immigration records. The annual commemoration makes Lares a center for Puerto Rican nationalist historical records.

county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Municipio of Las Marías, established 1871, carved from Mayagüez, Lares. Puerto Rico is a US territory (since 1898); previously a Spanish colony from 1508. Each municipio functions as a county-equivalent jurisdiction.
county_metadata.research_tips: Las Marías was carved from Mayagüez and Lares in 1871 in the western mountains. Known as 'La Ciudad de los Mangos.' Parroquia Nuestra Señora del Rosario holds records from the 1870s. Pre-1871 records divide between Mayagüez and Lares parishes. Coffee cultivation was the primary economy in this mountain municipio — check AGPR for coffee hacienda records, worker lists, and notarial protocols for property transactions. The remote mountain location meant limited population exchange with coastal areas. Barrios like Bucarabones, Espino, Furnias, and Maravilla have distinct settlement histories. Civil registry from 1885 provides vital records for the late Spanish period.

county_metadata.record_losses: Hurricane María (2017) caused damage to municipal infrastructure in Las Piedras. Some local government records and documents may have been affected by flooding and wind damage.
county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Municipio of Las Piedras, established 1801, carved from Humacao. Puerto Rico is a US territory (since 1898); previously a Spanish colony from 1508. Each municipio functions as a county-equivalent jurisdiction.
county_metadata.research_tips: Las Piedras was carved from Humacao in 1801 in the eastern interior. Known for its rocky terrain (piedras = stones). Parroquia Nuestra Señora del Rosario holds records from the early 19th century. Pre-1801 records are in Humacao's parish archives. Tobacco and sugar cultivation generated hacienda records at AGPR. The municipio served as a transit point between the coast and the interior — merchant and transportation records are genealogically useful. Barrios like Boquerón, Ceiba, El Río, Montones, and Tejas have distinct settlement patterns. Check notarial protocols for land division records. Las Piedras families frequently intermarried with Humacao, Juncos, and Naguabo families.

county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Municipio of Loíza, established 1719, one of Puerto Rico's original settlements. Puerto Rico is a US territory (since 1898); previously a Spanish colony from 1508. Each municipio functions as a county-equivalent jurisdiction.
county_metadata.research_tips: Loíza, founded as a parish around 1719, has the deepest African-descended heritage in Puerto Rico. Named after the Taíno cacica Yuiza. Parroquia Espíritu Santo and Parroquia San Patricio hold records from the 18th century — these are critical sources for Afro-Puerto Rican genealogy. Enslaved persons records, slave registers, coartación documents, and liberto records at AGPR are extensive. The Festival de Santiago Apóstol reflects Afro-Caribbean cultural traditions. As parent to Canóvanas (1909) and Río Grande (1840), Loíza records serve a wide area. The aldea system (small settlements) creates barrio-level record groupings. Coconut plantation records provide occupational genealogical data.

source_profiles.source_name: Loíza Slave Registers and Abolition Records (1872-1873)
source_profiles.repository: Archivo General de Puerto Rico (AGPR), San Juan
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Slave registers (registros de esclavos) for Loíza including the 1872 census of enslaved persons mandated before the 1873 Abolition Law. Also includes coartación (self-purchase) agreements, liberto (freed persons) records, and slave sale documents in notarial protocols. Essential for tracing African-descended families.
source_profiles.survival_notes: The 1872 slave register is generally well-preserved at AGPR. Earlier slave records are scattered across notarial protocols, church registers, and estate inventories.

county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Municipio of Luquillo, established 1797, carved from Fajardo. Puerto Rico is a US territory (since 1898); previously a Spanish colony from 1508. Each municipio functions as a county-equivalent jurisdiction.
county_metadata.research_tips: Luquillo was carved from Fajardo in 1797 on the northeast coast at the foot of El Yunque rainforest. Parroquia San José holds records from the late 18th century. Pre-1797 records are in Fajardo's parish archives. The El Yunque National Forest (formerly Caribbean National Forest) created US Forest Service administrative records at NARA from 1903. Fishing and agriculture (coconut, sugar) generated diverse occupational records. The Sabana River valley settlements predate the municipio. Hurricane damage records are particularly extensive — Luquillo's exposed northeast location made it vulnerable. Check Fajardo church registers for pre-1797 families. Barrios like Juan Martín, Mata de Plátano, and Pitahaya have colonial-era settlement records.

county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Municipio of Manatí, established 1738, one of Puerto Rico's original settlements. Puerto Rico is a US territory (since 1898); previously a Spanish colony from 1508. Each municipio functions as a county-equivalent jurisdiction.
county_metadata.research_tips: Manatí, founded as a parish around 1738, is a major north-coast city. Known as 'La Atenas de Puerto Rico' for its cultural and educational contributions. Parroquia Nuestra Señora de la Candelaria holds records from the mid-18th century. As parent or co-parent to Morovis (1818) and other interior municipios, Manatí records serve a broad north-central area. Sugar plantations generated extensive enslaved persons records. The Hacienda La Esperanza (now a nature reserve) has well-documented records at the Conservation Trust of Puerto Rico. Notarial protocols at AGPR document the complex sugar economy. Check colonial-era padrones for early population listings.

source_profiles.source_name: Manatí Spanish Colonial Census Records (Padrones)
source_profiles.repository: Archivo General de Puerto Rico (AGPR), San Juan / Archivo Histórico Nacional, Madrid
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Spanish colonial census records (padrones) for Manatí. Include population counts by race, age, occupation, and household. Key padrones: 1765, 1776, 1800, 1812, 1820, 1846, 1860, 1887, 1897. Some padrones list individuals by name; others are statistical summaries.
source_profiles.survival_notes: Survival varies by year. The 1800, 1860, and 1897 padrones are among the most complete. Some early padrones survive only as summaries at the Archivo Histórico Nacional in Madrid.

source_profiles.source_name: Manatí Property Registry (Registro de la Propiedad)
source_profiles.repository: Registro de la Propiedad, Manatí district
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Property registry records for Manatí district. The Spanish mortgage law system (Ley Hipotecaria) was established in Puerto Rico in 1870, creating a formal property registration system that continues today. Records document property ownership, transfers, mortgages, and liens.
source_profiles.survival_notes: Property registry records are generally well-preserved from 1870. The formal registration system provides continuous chains of title.

source_profiles.source_name: Manatí Municipal Cemetery Records (Cementerio Municipal)
source_profiles.repository: Municipio de Manatí / Find A Grave
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Municipal cemetery records for Manatí. Municipal cemeteries were established in most Puerto Rico municipios during the 19th century under Spanish health regulations. Records include burial registers, grave assignments, and monument inscriptions. Find A Grave has partial crowdsourced transcriptions.
source_profiles.survival_notes: Municipal cemetery records vary in completeness. Earlier burials were in church cemeteries (camposantos). Some municipal cemetery records were damaged by hurricanes.

source_profiles.source_name: Manatí Slave Registers and Abolition Records (1872-1873)
source_profiles.repository: Archivo General de Puerto Rico (AGPR), San Juan
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Slave registers (registros de esclavos) for Manatí including the 1872 census of enslaved persons mandated before the 1873 Abolition Law. Also includes coartación (self-purchase) agreements, liberto (freed persons) records, and slave sale documents in notarial protocols. Essential for tracing African-descended families.
source_profiles.survival_notes: The 1872 slave register is generally well-preserved at AGPR. Earlier slave records are scattered across notarial protocols, church registers, and estate inventories.

county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Municipio of Maricao, established 1874, carved from San Germán, Mayagüez. Puerto Rico is a US territory (since 1898); previously a Spanish colony from 1508. Each municipio functions as a county-equivalent jurisdiction.
county_metadata.research_tips: Maricao was carved from San Germán and Mayagüez in 1874 in the western mountains. Known for its coffee production and the Bosque Estatal de Maricao (state forest). Parroquia Nuestra Señora de la Monserrate holds records from the 1870s. Pre-1874 records divide between San Germán and Mayagüez parishes. Coffee hacienda records at AGPR are the primary genealogical source for this mountain municipio. Corsican and Mallorcan immigrants established many coffee farms in the late 19th century. The Monte del Estado area has distinct settlement patterns. Maricao's remote mountain location preserved traditional farming communities — barrio-level research using notarial protocols is essential.

source_profiles.source_name: Maricao Catholic Parish Registers (Baptisms, Marriages, Burials)
source_profiles.repository: Parish Archives / FamilySearch / AGPR, San Juan
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Catholic parish registers for Maricao municipio including baptisms (bautismos), marriages (matrimonios), and burials (defunciones). FamilySearch has digitized microfilm for many Puerto Rico parishes. Coverage begins circa 1874.
source_profiles.survival_notes: Parish registers are the primary genealogical source for Puerto Rico. Some early volumes deteriorated due to tropical climate. FamilySearch microfilm provides backup copies.

source_profiles.source_name: Maricao Civil Registry (Registro Demográfico) — Births, Marriages, Deaths
source_profiles.repository: Registro Demográfico, Maricao / AGPR, San Juan
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Civil registry records for Maricao including births, marriages, and deaths from 1885. Spanish civil registration began 1885 under Royal Decree. US administration continued the system after 1898.
source_profiles.survival_notes: Civil registry records generally well-preserved from 1885. Some gaps in early years due to incomplete compliance. Tropical climate and hurricanes have affected some volumes.

source_profiles.source_name: Maricao Notarial Protocols (Protocolos Notariales)
source_profiles.repository: Archivo General de Puerto Rico (AGPR), San Juan
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Notarial protocols for Maricao district covering property transfers, testaments, powers of attorney, marriage contracts, slave sales, coartación agreements, and other legal instruments from 1874. Spanish legal tradition required notarized documents for most transactions.
source_profiles.survival_notes: Notarial protocols at AGPR are generally well-preserved. Some volumes from the earliest periods show deterioration. AGPR's climate-controlled storage protects these irreplaceable records.

county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Municipio of Maunabo, established 1799, carved from Yabucoa. Puerto Rico is a US territory (since 1898); previously a Spanish colony from 1508. Each municipio functions as a county-equivalent jurisdiction.
county_metadata.research_tips: Maunabo was carved from Yabucoa in 1799 on the southeastern tip of Puerto Rico. Known for the Punta Tuna lighthouse (Faro de Punta Tuna). Parroquia San Antonio de Padua holds records from the late 18th century. Pre-1799 records are in Yabucoa's parish archives. Sugar plantations along the coast generated enslaved persons records and hacienda documentation at AGPR. The lighthouse keeper records at NARA and AGPR document families associated with coastal navigation. Maunabo's relative isolation meant families remained in barrios for generations. Check notarial protocols for coastal land transactions. The fishing economy created boat registration and fishing permit records distinct from agricultural records.

source_profiles.survival_notes: Parish registers are the primary genealogical source for Puerto Rico. Some early volumes deteriorated due to tropical climate. FamilySearch microfilm provides backup copies.

county_metadata.record_losses: 1918 earthquake and tsunami damaged some municipal records and buildings. Hurricane María (2017) also caused damage.
county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Municipio of Mayagüez, established 1763, one of Puerto Rico's original settlements. Puerto Rico is a US territory (since 1898); previously a Spanish colony from 1508. Each municipio functions as a county-equivalent jurisdiction.
county_metadata.research_tips: Mayagüez, established as a parish around 1763, is the largest west-coast city and home to the University of Puerto Rico at Mayagüez (RUM). Parroquia Nuestra Señora de la Candelaria holds records from the mid-18th century. As parent to Hormigueros (1874), Las Marías (1871), and co-parent to Maricao, Mayagüez records cover a wide western region. Major port city — shipping manifests, customs records, and passenger lists at AGPR and NARA document maritime commerce and immigration. The 1918 earthquake and tsunami destroyed some records and buildings. French, Corsican, and other European immigrants are well-documented. The needlework industry (1920s-1950s) created distinctive employment records.

source_profiles.source_name: Mayagüez Catholic Parish Registers (Baptisms, Marriages, Burials)
source_profiles.repository: Parish Archives / FamilySearch / AGPR, San Juan
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Catholic parish registers for Mayagüez municipio including baptisms (bautismos), marriages (matrimonios), and burials (defunciones). FamilySearch has digitized microfilm for many Puerto Rico parishes. Coverage begins circa 1763.
source_profiles.survival_notes: Parish registers are the primary genealogical source for Puerto Rico. Some early volumes deteriorated due to tropical climate. FamilySearch microfilm provides backup copies.

source_profiles.source_name: Mayagüez Historical Newspapers
source_profiles.repository: Colección Puertorriqueña, UPR / Biblioteca Nacional / Chronicling America
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Historical newspapers published in or covering Mayagüez. Puerto Rico's newspaper tradition began in the early 19th century. Papers include birth/death/marriage announcements, legal notices, estate settlements, and community news. La Gaceta de Puerto Rico (official gazette) published government notices.
source_profiles.survival_notes: Some newspaper runs are incomplete. The Colección Puertorriqueña at UPR Río Piedras has the most extensive holdings. Chronicling America has digitized selected Puerto Rico newspapers.
